questions.question: What is JWT (JSON Web Token) and how does it work?
questions.answer:
JWT is a compact, self-contained token format used to securely transmit information between parties as a JSON object. It's the standard for stateless authentication in REST APIs.

Structure: Three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature

  • Header — algorithm + token type: {"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}
  • Payload — claims (user data): {"sub":"user123","name":"Darsh","role":"Admin","exp":1234567890}
  • Signature — HMACSHA256(base64(header) + "." + base64(payload), secretKey) — verifies token wasn't tampered with

Authentication flow:

  1. User sends credentials → API validates → issues JWT
  2. Client stores JWT (localStorage or httpOnly cookie)
  3. Client sends JWT in Authorization: Bearer <token> header on every request
  4. API validates signature (no DB call needed!) + checks expiry → extracts claims

In FolioTrack, JWTs contained: sub (userId), email, role, exp (15 min). Refresh tokens (stored in httpOnly cookies, DB-tracked) issued new access tokens without re-login.
followUps: What are the standard JWT claims?
What is the difference between signing and encryption?
How do you invalidate a JWT before expiry?

followUpAnswers.What are the standard JWT claims?:
JWT defines a set of registered claim names in RFC 7519. These are not mandatory but have well-understood meanings:

ClaimFull NameDescription
issIssuerWho issued the token (e.g., https://myapi.com)
subSubjectWho the token is about — typically the user ID
audAudienceWho the token is intended for (e.g., https://myapi.com/api)
expExpiration TimeUnix timestamp after which the token is invalid
nbfNot BeforeUnix timestamp before which the token is invalid
iatIssued AtUnix timestamp when the token was issued
jtiJWT IDUnique identifier for this token (useful for revocation)

Beyond registered claims, OIDC adds public claims like email, name, given_name, picture. You can also add your own private claims (e.g., role, tenant_id).

// Reading registered claims in ASP.NET Core
var userId = User.FindFirstValue(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Sub);
var email = User.FindFirstValue(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Email);
var issuedAt = User.FindFirstValue(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Iat);

// Custom claim
var tenantId = User.FindFirstValue("tenant_id");

Key interview point: exp is a Unix epoch number (seconds since 1970-01-01 UTC), not a date string. ASP.NET Core's JWT middleware validates it automatically when ValidateLifetime = true.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between signing a:
These two concepts are often confused but serve entirely different purposes in JWT security:

Signing (JWS — JSON Web Signature)

  • The payload is visible — Base64URL-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone can decode and read it.
  • The signature proves the token wasn't tampered with and was issued by a trusted party.
  • Common algorithms: HS256 (HMAC-SHA256, symmetric — same key signs and verifies), RS256 (RSA, asymmetric — private key signs, public key verifies).
  • Use case: API authentication where the API just needs to verify identity claims. The payload data is not secret (it's a user ID and role, not a password).

Encryption (JWE — JSON Web Encryption)

  • The payload is encrypted — the content cannot be read without the decryption key.
  • Provides confidentiality: even if someone intercepts the token, they can't read its contents.
  • Use case: Tokens that carry sensitive data (SSN, medical data) that must not be readable in transit or by the client.

What most apps use: Signed JWTs (JWS) over HTTPS. HTTPS handles transport-layer confidentiality; JWT signing handles integrity and authenticity. A JWS token over plain HTTP is vulnerable — anyone can read the claims (though they can't forge them).

// HS256 — symmetric: same secret signs and verifies
// Use when only YOUR API validates tokens
var key = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(secret));
var creds = new SigningCredentials(key, SecurityAlgorithms.HmacSha256);

// RS256 — asymmetric: private key signs, public key verifies
// Use when multiple services need to verify tokens (they only get the public key)
var rsa = RSA.Create();
rsa.ImportFromPem(privateKeyPem);
var rsaKey = new RsaSecurityKey(rsa);
var creds = new SigningCredentials(rsaKey, SecurityAlgorithms.RsaSha256);

Interview tip: In a microservices architecture, prefer RS256. Each service only needs the public key to verify tokens — you never distribute the signing secret to every service.
followUpAnswers.How do you invalidate a JWT before expir:
This is the core tension in JWT design: JWTs are stateless by design, which means the server has no record of issued tokens to invalidate. If the secret hasn't changed, a valid JWT remains valid until exp. Here are the accepted strategies:

1. Short expiry (primary defense)
Set access tokens to expire in 5–15 minutes. A stolen token is only valid for that window. Pair with longer-lived refresh tokens for UX.

2. Token blocklist (denylist)
Maintain a set of revoked jti (JWT IDs) in Redis or in-memory cache. Check every incoming request against the blocklist.

// Store revoked JTI in Redis on logout
public async Task RevokeToken(string jti, DateTime expiry)
{
    var ttl = expiry - DateTime.UtcNow;
    await _redis.StringSetAsync($"revoked:{jti}", "1", ttl);
}

// In a custom JWT event handler
options.Events = new JwtBearerEvents
{
    OnTokenValidated = async ctx =>
    {
        var jti = ctx.Principal!.FindFirstValue(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Jti);
        if (await _redis.KeyExistsAsync($"revoked:{jti}"))
            ctx.Fail("Token has been revoked");
    }
};

3. Refresh token rotation
The access token is short-lived (15 min). The refresh token is stored in the DB and can be deleted/invalidated server-side on logout. After logout the access token expires naturally in ≤15 min.

4. Signing key rotation
Change the signing secret — all previously issued tokens instantly become invalid. Nuclear option — only for security incidents.

5. Version claim
Add a token_version claim to each JWT that matches a user DB field. Incrementing the DB version (on password change, logout-all) invalidates all tokens for that user.

// In token validation — check version against DB
OnTokenValidated = async ctx =>
{
    var userId = ctx.Principal!.FindFirstValue(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Sub);
    var tokenVersion = ctx.Principal.FindFirstValue("token_version");
    var user = await _db.Users.FindAsync(userId);
    if (user?.TokenVersion.ToString() != tokenVersion)
        ctx.Fail("Token version invalid");
};

Recommended approach for most apps: Short access token (15 min) + refresh token in DB. On logout, delete the refresh token. Accept the 15-min window for the access token — it's an acceptable trade-off for statelessness.

questions.tip: JWTs cannot be invalidated server-side (they're stateless). Keep access token expiry short (15min). Use refresh tokens in DB to support logout/revocation — revoke the refresh token, and the access token expires naturally.

questions.question: How do you implement JWT authentication in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Setting up JWT authentication in ASP.NET Core 8:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddAuthentication(JwtBearerDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)
    .AddJwtBearer(options =>
    {
        options.TokenValidationParameters = new TokenValidationParameters
        {
            ValidateIssuer = true,
            ValidIssuer = builder.Configuration["Jwt:Issuer"],
            ValidateAudience = true,
            ValidAudience = builder.Configuration["Jwt:Audience"],
            ValidateLifetime = true,
            ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true,
            IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(
                Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(builder.Configuration["Jwt:SecretKey"]!)),
            ClockSkew = TimeSpan.Zero  // no tolerance for expired tokens
        };
    });

// Token generation
public string GenerateToken(ApplicationUser user)
{
    var claims = new[]
    {
        new Claim(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Sub, user.Id),
        new Claim(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Email, user.Email!),
        new Claim(ClaimTypes.Role, "User")
    };
    var key = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(_secretKey));
    var token = new JwtSecurityToken(
        issuer: _issuer,
        audience: _audience,
        claims: claims,
        expires: DateTime.UtcNow.AddMinutes(15),
        signingCredentials: new SigningCredentials(key, SecurityAlgorithms.HmacSha256));
    return new JwtSecurityTokenHandler().WriteToken(token);
}
followUps: How do you read claims in a controller?

followUpAnswers.How do you read claims in a controller?:
Once the JWT middleware validates a token, it populates HttpContext.User with a ClaimsPrincipal. In a controller, this is exposed as the User property (inherited from ControllerBase).

[ApiController]
[Authorize]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class PortfolioController : ControllerBase
{
    // Get the user ID (from the 'sub' claim)
    private string GetUserId() =>
        User.FindFirstValue(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Sub)
        ?? User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier)
        ?? throw new UnauthorizedAccessException();

    [HttpGet]
    public async Task<IActionResult> GetMyPortfolios()
    {
        var userId = GetUserId();
        var email = User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.Email);
        var role = User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.Role);
        var tenantId = User.FindFirstValue("tenant_id");  // custom claim

        // Role check via method
        if (User.IsInRole("Admin"))
        {
            // admin-specific logic
        }

        // All claims
        var allClaims = User.Claims.Select(c => new { c.Type, c.Value });

        var portfolios = await _service.GetForUser(userId);
        return Ok(portfolios);
    }
}

Claim type gotcha: The JWT sub claim is mapped to ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier by default in ASP.NET Core's JWT middleware. If you add the claim using JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Sub, look for it with either constant. To disable this mapping and keep the original claim names:

// In Program.cs — preserve original JWT claim names
JwtSecurityTokenHandler.DefaultInboundClaimTypeMap.Clear();

In Minimal APIs, use HttpContext.User directly or inject ClaimsPrincipal as a parameter:

app.MapGet("/me", (ClaimsPrincipal user) =>
{
    var id = user.FindFirstValue("sub");
    return Results.Ok(new { id });
}).RequireAuthorization();
followUpAnswers.What is ClaimsPrincipal?:
ClaimsPrincipal is the .NET abstraction for the currently authenticated user. It models the fact that a user can have multiple identities (e.g., logged in with both a local account and an external provider simultaneously).

Object hierarchy:

  • ClaimsPrincipal — the user. Has one or more ClaimsIdentity objects.
  • ClaimsIdentity — a single identity source (e.g., the JWT). Has a collection of Claim objects and an AuthenticationType (e.g., "Bearer").
  • Claim — a key-value fact: Type (e.g., "sub") and Value (e.g., "user-123").
// Anatomy of ClaimsPrincipal
ClaimsPrincipal principal = HttpContext.User;

// The primary identity (first authenticated one)
ClaimsIdentity? identity = principal.Identity as ClaimsIdentity;
bool isAuthenticated = identity?.IsAuthenticated ?? false;
string? authType = identity?.AuthenticationType; // "Bearer" for JWT

// All identities (if user logged in with multiple schemes)
IEnumerable<ClaimsIdentity> allIdentities = principal.Identities;

// Claims from ALL identities
IEnumerable<Claim> allClaims = principal.Claims;

// Find a specific claim (searches all identities)
string? userId = principal.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier);

// Role check (also searches all identities)
bool isAdmin = principal.IsInRole("Admin");

Where it comes from: The JWT middleware reads the Authorization: Bearer <token> header, validates the token, parses the claims, constructs a ClaimsIdentity, wraps it in a ClaimsPrincipal, and sets it on HttpContext.User. All of this happens in the authentication middleware before your controller runs.

In tests, you can mock it directly:

var claims = new[] { new Claim("sub", "test-user-id") };
var identity = new ClaimsIdentity(claims, "Bearer");
var principal = new ClaimsPrincipal(identity);
controllerUnderTest.ControllerContext = new ControllerContext
{
    HttpContext = new DefaultHttpContext { User = principal }
};

questions.tip: Read claims in controllers: User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier) gets the user ID. User.IsInRole("Admin") checks roles. User.Claims for all claims. The User property is a ClaimsPrincipal populated by the JWT middleware.

questions.question: What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization?
questions.answer:
These are different steps in the security pipeline that are often confused:

Authentication — verifying WHO the user is. "Are you who you claim to be?" Methods: username+password, JWT, OAuth, biometrics. Result: a verified identity (ClaimsPrincipal in ASP.NET Core).

Authorization — verifying WHAT the authenticated user can do. "Are you allowed to do this?" Methods: roles, claims, policies, resource-based authorization.

// Authentication: verify identity
app.UseAuthentication();  // sets HttpContext.User from JWT

// Authorization: enforce what they can do
app.UseAuthorization();

// Role-based authorization
[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]
public IActionResult AdminOnly() { ... }

// Policy-based authorization
[Authorize(Policy = "CanEditPortfolio")]
public IActionResult Edit(int id) { ... }

// Register policy
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("CanEditPortfolio", policy =>
        policy.RequireClaim("PortfolioOwner"));
});

In WalletVerification: Authentication = validating the OIDC token from the identity provider. Authorization = checking that the authenticated user's sub claim matches the wallet they're trying to access (resource-based authorization).

followUpAnswers.What is resource-based authorization?:
Resource-based authorization is authorization that depends on the specific resource being accessed, not just the user's role or claims. You can't express it with a simple [Authorize(Policy = "...")] attribute because the policy evaluation needs to inspect the actual resource (loaded from the DB).

Classic example: A user can edit their own portfolio but not someone else's. The Portfolio resource has an OwnerId — authorization requires comparing it to the current user's ID.

// 1. Define a requirement
public class PortfolioOwnerRequirement : IAuthorizationRequirement { }

// 2. Implement a handler
public class PortfolioOwnerHandler
    : AuthorizationHandler<PortfolioOwnerRequirement, Portfolio>
{
    protected override Task HandleRequirementAsync(
        AuthorizationHandlerContext context,
        PortfolioOwnerRequirement requirement,
        Portfolio resource)
    {
        var userId = context.User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier);
        if (resource.OwnerId == userId)
            context.Succeed(requirement);
        // else: leave it unhandled — other handlers may succeed or it fails
        return Task.CompletedTask;
    }
}

// 3. Register
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IAuthorizationHandler, PortfolioOwnerHandler>();
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
    options.AddPolicy("PortfolioOwner",
        p => p.Requirements.Add(new PortfolioOwnerRequirement())));

// 4. Use in controller — cannot use [Authorize] attribute here
[HttpPut("{id}")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Update(int id, UpdateDto dto)
{
    var portfolio = await _repo.FindAsync(id);
    if (portfolio == null) return NotFound();

    var authResult = await _authorizationService
        .AuthorizeAsync(User, portfolio, "PortfolioOwner");

    if (!authResult.Succeeded)
        return Forbid();

    // proceed with update
}

Key distinction: Regular policies run before the resource is loaded. Resource-based authorization runs after — it requires the resource as context. Inject IAuthorizationService and call AuthorizeAsync(user, resource, policy) explicitly in the action method.
followUpAnswers.What is an IAuthorizationHandler?:
IAuthorizationHandler is the interface that contains the authorization logic for a specific IAuthorizationRequirement. ASP.NET Core's authorization system separates what is required (the requirement) from how to evaluate it (the handler).

Why separate them? You can have multiple handlers for the same requirement. If any handler succeeds and none explicitly fail, the requirement is met. This enables OR-logic: a user can pass authorization by satisfying any one of multiple conditions.

// Requirement: just a marker class (no logic)
public class MinimumAgeRequirement : IAuthorizationRequirement
{
    public int MinimumAge { get; }
    public MinimumAgeRequirement(int age) => MinimumAge = age;
}

// Handler: contains the logic
public class MinimumAgeHandler
    : AuthorizationHandler<MinimumAgeRequirement>
{
    protected override Task HandleRequirementAsync(
        AuthorizationHandlerContext context,
        MinimumAgeRequirement requirement)
    {
        var dobClaim = context.User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.DateOfBirth);
        if (dobClaim != null
            && DateTime.TryParse(dobClaim, out var dob)
            && DateTime.Today.Year - dob.Year >= requirement.MinimumAge)
        {
            context.Succeed(requirement);  // passes
        }
        // Don't call context.Fail() unless you want to hard-fail
        // (prevents other handlers from succeeding)
        return Task.CompletedTask;
    }
}

// Register both
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IAuthorizationHandler, MinimumAgeHandler>();
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("AtLeast18",
        p => p.Requirements.Add(new MinimumAgeRequirement(18)));
});

Handler lifetime: Register handlers as Singleton when they have no scoped dependencies. If a handler needs DbContext or other scoped services, register it as Scoped. The authorization framework resolves all registered handlers and evaluates them.

context.Succeed vs context.Fail:

  • context.Succeed(requirement) — marks the requirement as met. Authorization passes if all requirements succeed.
  • context.Fail() — hard fail; overrides any Succeed from other handlers. Use for explicit denial (e.g., account locked).
  • Doing nothing — leaves it to other handlers. If no handler succeeds, authorization fails.

questions.tip: HTTP 401 vs 403: Authentication failure → 401 Unauthorized ("who are you?"). Authorization failure → 403 Forbidden ("I know who you are, but you can't do this"). Using the wrong code leaks information — returning 403 for unauthenticated requests tells attackers the endpoint exists.

questions.question: What is OAuth 2.0 and how does it differ from OIDC?
questions.answer:
OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework — it allows a third-party app to get limited access to a user's resources on another service without exposing credentials. It's about delegated authorization, not authentication.

OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow:

  1. User clicks "Login with Google" in your app
  2. App redirects to Google's authorization endpoint
  3. User logs in to Google and consents to permissions
  4. Google redirects back with an authorization code
  5. App exchanges code for access token (server-to-server, no user involved)
  6. App uses access token to call Google APIs on behalf of the user

OpenID Connect (OIDC) — an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. Adds authentication: after the flow, you receive an ID Token (JWT) containing who the user is (sub, email, name). OAuth gives you access tokens to call APIs. OIDC adds identity tokens to know who logged in.

In WalletVerification I configured ASP.NET Core's OIDC handler to authenticate users via an external identity provider. The ID token told us who the user was; the access token let us call the provider's API.
followUps: What is PKCE and why is it required for SPAs?
What is the difference between access token and ID token?

followUpAnswers.What is PKCE and why is it required for:
PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange, pronounced "pixy") is an extension to the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow that protects against authorization code interception attacks. It's defined in RFC 7636 and is now mandatory for all public clients (SPAs, mobile apps).

The problem PKCE solves: In a traditional Authorization Code flow, the authorization code is returned as a URL parameter (in a redirect). If a malicious app on the same device intercepts this redirect, it can steal the code and exchange it for tokens — because the client secret is absent in a SPA.

How PKCE works:

  1. App generates a random code verifier (43–128 character random string)
  2. App hashes it with SHA-256 → code challenge
  3. App sends the code challenge with the authorization request
  4. Authorization server stores the code challenge alongside the authorization code
  5. When the app exchanges the code for tokens, it sends the original code verifier
  6. Authorization server hashes the verifier and compares to stored challenge — if they match, the exchange succeeds
// Angular/SPA implementation (using angular-oauth2-oidc)
import { AuthConfig } from 'angular-oauth2-oidc';

export const authConfig: AuthConfig = {
  issuer: 'https://identity.example.com',
  redirectUri: window.location.origin + '/callback',
  clientId: 'my-spa-client',
  responseType: 'code',       // Authorization Code flow
  scope: 'openid profile email',
  useSilentRefresh: true,
  // PKCE is enabled by default in modern OAuth libraries
  // The library handles code_verifier/code_challenge generation
};

Why SPAs can't use client secrets: A SPA runs entirely in the browser. Any client secret embedded in the JavaScript bundle is visible to anyone who opens DevTools. PKCE replaces the client secret with a cryptographic proof that only the initiating app can provide — each flow generates a fresh verifier.

In ASP.NET Core (confidential client — server-side app): The secret is kept server-side, so PKCE is optional but still recommended as defense-in-depth.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between access to:
Both are JWTs issued by an identity provider, but they serve fundamentally different purposes:

Access TokenID Token
PurposeAuthorize API callsAuthenticate the user (who logged in)
StandardOAuth 2.0OpenID Connect (OIDC)
AudienceThe resource server (your API)The client application
ContentsScopes, expiry — format is undefined by OAuthsub, email, name, iat, exp — OIDC-defined claims
Should the client read it?No (opaque to client in theory)Yes — this is how the client knows who logged in

Key rule: Never use an access token to identify a user. The OAuth 2.0 spec does not define what's inside an access token — it's for the API, not the app. Some providers put user info in it, others make it opaque. Use the ID token or the /userinfo endpoint instead.

// In ASP.NET Core OIDC, both tokens are available after authentication
var idToken = await HttpContext.GetTokenAsync("id_token");
var accessToken = await HttpContext.GetTokenAsync("access_token");

// User identity comes from the ID token claims (set on HttpContext.User)
var userId = User.FindFirstValue("sub");     // from ID token
var email = User.FindFirstValue("email");   // from ID token

// Use access token to call a downstream API
httpClient.DefaultRequestHeaders.Authorization =
    new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", accessToken);

Flow summary:

  • OIDC flow returns: id_token + access_token + optionally refresh_token
  • The id_token tells your app "the user who just logged in is Alice (sub=abc123)"
  • The access_token is what you pass to APIs (including /userinfo) to act on Alice's behalf

questions.tip: Never use the access token to get user identity. Access tokens are for calling APIs — their content is undefined by OAuth. Use the ID token (OIDC) or a /userinfo endpoint for identity. This is a common OAuth misuse.

questions.question: What is ASP.NET Core Identity and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core Identity is the built-in membership system for managing users, passwords, roles, and claims. It provides: user registration/login, password hashing (bcrypt), email confirmation, 2FA, roles, claims, external login (Google, Facebook via OAuth).

// Setup
builder.Services.AddDefaultIdentity<ApplicationUser>(options =>
{
    options.Password.RequiredLength = 8;
    options.Password.RequireNonAlphanumeric = false;
    options.SignIn.RequireConfirmedAccount = false;
    options.Lockout.MaxFailedAccessAttempts = 5;
})
.AddRoles<IdentityRole>()
.AddEntityFrameworkStores<ApplicationDbContext>();

// Usage in controller
public async Task<IActionResult> Register(RegisterViewModel model)
{
    var user = new ApplicationUser { UserName = model.Email, Email = model.Email };
    var result = await _userManager.CreateAsync(user, model.Password);
    if (result.Succeeded)
    {
        await _signInManager.SignInAsync(user, isPersistent: false);
        return RedirectToAction("Index", "Home");
    }
    foreach (var error in result.Errors)
        ModelState.AddModelError(string.Empty, error.Description);
    return View(model);
}

When to use: Self-hosted authentication where you own the user database. If you need external identity providers (Azure AD, Auth0), use OIDC instead and skip Identity's password management.
followUps: What is the difference between UserManager and SignInManager?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between UserManag:
Both are core services in ASP.NET Core Identity but they operate at different levels:

UserManager<TUser>SignInManager<TUser>
ConcernUser data managementAuthentication session management
Works withThe user store (database)HTTP cookies, claims identity
HTTP context needed?No — can be used in background servicesYes — reads/writes cookies on HttpContext

UserManager — CRUD for users and their associated data:

// Create a user
var result = await _userManager.CreateAsync(user, password);

// Find user
var user = await _userManager.FindByEmailAsync(email);
var user = await _userManager.GetUserAsync(User); // from HttpContext.User

// Password operations
bool isValid = await _userManager.CheckPasswordAsync(user, password);
var result = await _userManager.ChangePasswordAsync(user, oldPwd, newPwd);
var token = await _userManager.GeneratePasswordResetTokenAsync(user);
var result = await _userManager.ResetPasswordAsync(user, token, newPassword);

// Claims and roles
await _userManager.AddClaimAsync(user, new Claim("subscription", "premium"));
await _userManager.AddToRoleAsync(user, "Admin");
var roles = await _userManager.GetRolesAsync(user);

// Lockout
await _userManager.AccessFailedAsync(user);  // increment failed count
await _userManager.ResetAccessFailedCountAsync(user);

SignInManager — manages the authentication cookie and sign-in state:

// Sign in (sets the authentication cookie)
var result = await _signInManager.PasswordSignInAsync(
    email, password,
    isPersistent: rememberMe,
    lockoutOnFailure: true);

if (result.Succeeded) { /* redirect */ }
if (result.IsLockedOut) { /* show lockout view */ }
if (result.RequiresTwoFactor) { /* redirect to 2FA */ }

// Sign out (clears the cookie)
await _signInManager.SignOutAsync();

// External login (OAuth callback)
var info = await _signInManager.GetExternalLoginInfoAsync();
var result = await _signInManager.ExternalLoginSignInAsync(
    info.LoginProvider, info.ProviderKey, isPersistent: false);

Rule of thumb: Use UserManager anywhere (including background jobs, services). Use SignInManager only in controllers/Razor Pages where there's an HTTP context with cookies.
followUpAnswers.How does Identity store passwords?:
ASP.NET Core Identity uses PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) with HMAC-SHA512, not plain bcrypt — though the design is similarly adaptive.

Identity's password hashing (V3 format, .NET Core 2.0+):

  • Algorithm: PBKDF2 with HMAC-SHA512
  • Salt: 128-bit cryptographically random salt (unique per password)
  • Iterations: 100,000 by default (configurable)
  • Output: 256-bit derived key
  • Storage format: a single Base64 string with a version header, salt, iteration count, and hash
// How Identity handles passwords internally (conceptual)
// You never call this directly — Identity does it for you

// When creating a user:
await _userManager.CreateAsync(user, "MyPassword123!");
// Internally: generates random salt, runs PBKDF2, stores result in AspNetUsers.PasswordHash

// When verifying:
await _userManager.CheckPasswordAsync(user, inputPassword);
// Internally: reads stored hash, extracts salt and iterations,
// runs PBKDF2 on inputPassword with same params, compares results

// You can configure iterations (in .NET 8):
builder.Services.Configure<PasswordHasherOptions>(options =>
    options.IterationCount = 600_000); // OWASP 2023 recommendation for PBKDF2-SHA512

Why this is secure:

  • Salting — each password gets a unique salt, preventing rainbow table attacks and ensuring two users with the same password have different hashes
  • Key stretching — 100,000 iterations makes brute-forcing slow (attacker must run 100K hash operations per guess)
  • Adaptive — you can increase the iteration count as hardware gets faster, and Identity's verifier can upgrade old hashes automatically

Never implement your own password hashing. MD5, SHA1, SHA256, and even bcrypt-without-salt are all insufficient for passwords. Use Identity's IPasswordHasher<TUser> directly if you need password hashing outside of Identity.

questions.tip: Identity uses bcrypt for password hashing (via ASP.NET Core Data Protection). Never store plain passwords or use MD5/SHA1 — Identity's default is already secure. The hash is adaptive (work factor increases over time).

questions.question: What are claims and how are they used in ASP.NET Core authorization?
questions.answer:
A claim is a key-value statement about a user made by a trusted authority. Claims are carried in tokens (JWT, cookies) and represent facts about the user.

Common claims: sub (subject/user ID), email, role, given_name, custom claims like tenant_id, subscription_level.

Claims-based authorization:

// Policy requiring a specific claim
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("PremiumUser", policy =>
        policy.RequireClaim("subscription", "premium", "enterprise"));

    options.AddPolicy("MinAge18", policy =>
        policy.RequireAssertion(ctx =>
        {
            var dob = ctx.User.FindFirstValue("birthdate");
            return dob != null && DateTime.Parse(dob) <= DateTime.Now.AddYears(-18);
        }));
});

// Apply to controller/action
[Authorize(Policy = "PremiumUser")]
public IActionResult PremiumFeature() { ... }

// Read claims in controller
var userId = User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier);
var email = User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.Email);
var tenantId = User.FindFirstValue("tenant_id");

In WalletVerification, the JWT contained a wallet_id claim. Authorization policies verified that the requested wallet ID matched the claim — preventing users from accessing other wallets.
followUps: What is the difference between role-based and claim-based authorization?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between role-base:
Both are built into ASP.NET Core but they differ in flexibility and granularity:

Role-based authorization — binary membership check. A user either is or isn't in a role. Roles are coarse-grained: "Admin", "User", "Manager".

// Role-based — simple but inflexible
[Authorize(Roles = "Admin,Manager")]
public IActionResult ManageUsers() { ... }

// In code
if (User.IsInRole("Admin")) { ... }

// Behind the scenes, roles are just a special claim:
// ClaimTypes.Role = "http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/role"
// So role-based IS claim-based, just with a specific claim type

Claim-based authorization — richer, more expressive. Claims can carry values, not just presence/absence. Enables fine-grained rules:

// Claim-based — flexible
[Authorize(Policy = "CanPublishArticles")]
public IActionResult Publish() { ... }

// Policies can encode complex logic:
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    // Must have the claim with a specific value
    options.AddPolicy("CanPublishArticles",
        p => p.RequireClaim("permission", "articles.publish"));

    // Multiple conditions
    options.AddPolicy("SeniorEditor", p =>
    {
        p.RequireRole("Editor");               // must be Editor
        p.RequireClaim("tenure_years");        // and have this claim
        p.RequireAssertion(ctx =>
        {
            var years = ctx.User.FindFirstValue("tenure_years");
            return int.TryParse(years, out var y) && y >= 3;
        });
    });
});

When to use each:

  • Roles: Simple access control with a small, fixed set of roles. Fast to implement. Good for: admin vs user, internal vs external.
  • Claims: When authorization depends on attributes (subscription tier, department, permissions list, geographic region). Claims scale better in complex systems — you can add a new claim without changing the role hierarchy.
  • Policies (combines both): Always prefer policies over direct role checks in controllers — policies are testable, centralized, and can evolve without touching controllers.
followUpAnswers.What is an IAuthorizationRequirement?:
IAuthorizationRequirement is a marker interface — it has no methods. It's used purely as a type constraint that connects a requirement definition to its handler(s). Together, requirements and handlers form ASP.NET Core's custom authorization policy system.

// A requirement is just a data container — what conditions must be met
public class SubscriptionRequirement : IAuthorizationRequirement
{
    public SubscriptionTier MinimumTier { get; }
    public SubscriptionRequirement(SubscriptionTier tier)
        => MinimumTier = tier;
}

// The handler contains the evaluation logic
public class SubscriptionHandler
    : AuthorizationHandler<SubscriptionRequirement>
{
    protected override Task HandleRequirementAsync(
        AuthorizationHandlerContext context,
        SubscriptionRequirement requirement)
    {
        var tierClaim = context.User.FindFirstValue("subscription_tier");
        if (Enum.TryParse<SubscriptionTier>(tierClaim, out var userTier)
            && userTier >= requirement.MinimumTier)
        {
            context.Succeed(requirement);
        }
        return Task.CompletedTask;
    }
}

// Wire up in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IAuthorizationHandler, SubscriptionHandler>();
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("RequiresPremium", policy =>
        policy.Requirements.Add(
            new SubscriptionRequirement(SubscriptionTier.Premium)));

    options.AddPolicy("RequiresEnterprise", policy =>
        policy.Requirements.Add(
            new SubscriptionRequirement(SubscriptionTier.Enterprise)));
});

// Use on controller
[Authorize(Policy = "RequiresPremium")]
public IActionResult PremiumReport() { ... }

Benefits of this pattern:

  • Reusability: One requirement class + one handler can power multiple policies with different parameters
  • Testability: Handlers are plain classes — unit test them with a mock AuthorizationHandlerContext
  • Separation of concerns: Policy definition (what's required) is separate from evaluation logic (how to determine if met)
  • Multiple handlers per requirement: Register multiple handlers for one requirement; any one succeeding is sufficient (OR logic)

questions.tip: Claims are extensible but keep them small. Every claim is in every request (in the JWT). Don't put large data in claims — put a user ID and look up details per-request from cache. A JWT with 50 claims is a red flag.
